segments.s565-g57: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause ('when') and governs the past tense verb بشنید (3rd pers. sing. past of شنیدن). پاسخ is the direct object 'the reply'; هم here is a small emphatic/temporal particle ('then/also') reinforcing immediacy. اندر زمان is an adverbial phrase ('in/at the moment') placed after the verb to mark time.
segments.s565-g58: ز (poetic 'from') marks source; پرده is the noun 'curtain/veil' from which one emerges. بیامد is the past tense 'came/forthcame'. بر here means 'toward' (directional), followed by the goal دژ 'fortress'. دوان is a participial/adverbial form 'running/rushing' that modifies the action (he came running toward the fortress).
segments.s565-g59: شنیده is a circumstantial participle ('having heard') placed before the subject; بران ('thereupon/on that') links the participle to the ensuing action. سرو سیمین is a nominal phrase ('the silvery cypress' — a poetic epithet used as the subject). بگفت is the past-tense verb 'he said' (3rd pers. sing.).
segments.s565-g60: که introduces a reported clause ('that'). خورشید is the subject 'the Sun'; ناهید را is the marked internal argument with را as the object/affected party; گشت is the past tense of گشتن meaning 'became/turned into', and جفت is the predicate noun meaning 'mate/pair' (i.e., 'became a mate for Nahid'). Word order places the predicate after گشت.
segments.s565-g61: ز بالا is a prepositional phrase 'from on high/from above'. و coordinates the next phrase, linking it as an additional circumstance. دیدار شاپور شاه is a genitive-like phrase 'the meeting/appearance of Shapur the king' (classical Persian often omits the ezafe; the two nouns form a name/title phrase).
segments.s565-g62: بگفت is the main reporting verb 'he said/recounted'. آنچ (classical form of آنچه) is a relative pronoun 'that which/what' functioning as the object of بگفت. آمد is past 'came/happened' and here means 'befell/occurred to'; به marks the experiencer/target, and تابنده ماه is the noun phrase 'the shining moon' (تابنده modifies ماه).
segments.s565-g41: This is a compressed, poetic clause. بیامد is simple past 3rd‑person singular “came.” The following phrase is inverted: زمین را uses the object marker را but here marks the goal/ground toward which an action is directed (literary order). به مژگان is a prepositional phrase “with/onto (her) eyelashes” and مژگان is the noun “eyelashes.” برفت here is a poetic past action “let fall / cast down (the lashes)” but its lexeme senses given in the list do not match this verbal use (see senses).
segments.s565-g42: سخن هرچ بشنید is a noun phrase + verb: سخن “speech/words” modified by هرچ “whatever/whatever that,” and بشنید is past third‑person “(she) heard.” با شاه گفت follows: با marks the recipient/target (“to/with the king”) and گفت is the past verb “said/told” (here used transitively, though the supplied lemma senses are noun senses—see senses). The clause therefore means “she told the king whatever she had heard.”
segments.s565-g43: ز گفتار او is a causal prepositional phrase “from / because of her speech/words.” شاد شد is the compound past/copular construction “became happy.” شهریار is the subject but appears after the predicate for poetic emphasis (verb–subject order is common in older/poetic Persian).
segments.s565-g44: بخندید is past tense “he laughed.” و links clauses “and.” دینار (object) and دادش (داد + ش object clitic) mean “(he) gave her dinars”; here هزار follows to quantify the dinars, so the line reads “he laughed and gave her a thousand dinars.” Note دادش contains the verb داد “gave” plus the enclitic ش “her.”
segments.s565-g45: دو یاره is “two companions/attendants” (دو = two, یاره = attendant/follower). یکی introduces one of them “one (of them)” and طوق و انگشتری lists the items “a necklace/collar and a ring.” The verb is omitted here (ellipsis of داد/did give) and understood from the prior line.
segments.s565-g46: ز دیبای چینی و از بربری is a material phrase introduced by two prepositions: ز and از both meaning “from/of,” listing fabrics. دیبای چینی = “Chinese brocade/silk” (دیبای = brocade, چینی = Chinese) and بربری names the other origin/quality (literary/ethnic adjective). The second noun’s head (cloth/garment) is omitted but understood.
segments.s565-g47: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed phrase “gave such an answer” (چنین = thus/so; داد پاسخ = gave an answer). که introduces the content of that answer (subordinate clause). با ماه روی is a descriptive prepositional phrase “with moon‑face” — a conventional Persian epithet meaning “with a face like the moon” (i.e. beautiful).
segments.s565-g48: به خوبی سخنها فراوان بگوی is an imperative/request: به خوبی “well” (here adverbial), سخنها “the words” (pl.), فراوان “plentifully/many” modifies how much to speak, and بگوی is the subjunctive/imperative form of گفتن “to say/speak” (“speak” or “say”), placed at the end as in Persian; the supplied lemma senses for بگوی are noun senses and do not match this verbal use (see senses).
segments.s565-g9: بشد is a past/result verb meaning 'came to be' or 'befell' (here: 'came upon'). خواب و آرام is a compound nominal phrase 'sleep and calm/peace' coordinated by و. زان خوب‌چهر is a demonstrative phrase 'that fair‑faced (one)' (زان used poetically as 'that/of that'), and the whole segment means 'sleep and peace came upon that fair‑faced one.'
segments.s565-g10: بر دایه شد is literal 'fell upon / befell the nurse' where بر = 'upon/onto' and شد is verbal 'happened / came to be' (here: 'befell' or 'was on'). با دلی پر ز مهر is an adjunct with با + an abstract noun دلی 'a heart' qualified by پر ز + noun 'full of ...' (پر ز مهر = 'full of love'), so 'the nurse was / found with a heart full of affection.'
segments.s565-g11: بدو گفت is 'she/he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto it' marking the indirect object). کین شاه خورشیدفش here names or points to a person: کین + شاه + خورشیدفش functions as a nominal unit ('this/that vengeance‑king Khurshidfash' — kīn used as an attributive element 'vengeance/vengeful'). Note گفت is used in its reported‑speech sense though the provided sense list is nominal; grammatically it's the past reporting verb.
segments.s565-g12: که is the relative/complementizer 'who/that' introducing a clause describing the king. ایدر بیامد is 'has come here' (ایدر = 'here', بیامد = past 'came'). چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the following noun, and کینه‌کش is a descriptive noun 'one who carries/harbours vengeance' (a vindictive person). The clause reads 'who has come here so vindictive.'
segments.s565-g13: بزرگی او is 'his greatness' (noun + pronoun). چون here is a comparatival/parenthetical 'as/like; as it were'. نهان is an adjective 'hidden, concealed'. منست (from منس) is a predicative adjective meaning 'proper/appropriate' or 'well‑set'; together the segment says roughly 'his greatness, as it were, is concealed / concealed as befits him.'
segments.s565-g14: جهان خوانمش is a verbal phrase where خوانمش (خوان + ـمش) here has the sense 'I call/name him' (the speaker wondering what title to give). کو is the interrogative/exclamatory 'where / where is', and the repeated جهان ... منست asks rhetorically 'where is the world that is fitting?' — i.e. 'where is a world worthy of him?'. The clauses are rhetorical: 'Shall I call him “the world”? where is such a world that would be fitting?'
segments.s565-g15: پیامی ز من is 'a message from me' (پیامی = a message, ز = from, من = me). نزد شاپور بر uses نزد + name to mean 'to/at the presence of Shapur', and بر here functions as a directional/pointing element 'to/toward' (poetic word order places the phrase after the message). The segment means 'a message from me to Shapur.'
segments.s565-g16: به رزم آمدست is a perfective/periphrastic clause 'he has come to the battle' (به = to, رزم = battle, آمدست = 'has come'). او is the subject 'he'. ز من سور بر is an ablative/causal phrase 'on my account / because of me' (ز من = 'for/from me') plus سور بر 'he held a feast' (سور = feast, بر used here as a verbal particle with the sense 'upon/for'), so the whole second half reads 'he has come to battle; he held a feast on my account.'
segments.s565-g25: بدو دایه گفت = 'he said to the nurse.' بدو is the dative pronoun 'to her/him' placed before the verb (typical older/poetic word order). گفت is the past tense verb 'said.' آنچ فرمان دهی is a subordinate clause 'that which you command/whatever you command' where آنچ = 'that which/whatever,' فرمان = 'command,' and دهی is the present/subjunctive second-person form of 'to give' used here as 'you command.'
segments.s565-g26: بگویم بیارمت زو آگهی = two coordinated promises. بگویم here functions as 'I will tell' / 'I will say' (first-person, prospective sense); بیارمت is a compound verb with the object clitic -ت ('you') and the speaker's ending -م: 'I will bring you.' ز + (او) = 'from him' (here written as separate elements), and آگهی = 'information/news'; together z(o) آگهی means 'so you may be informed by him' or 'with news from him.'
segments.s565-g27: چو شب در زمین پادشاهی گرفت = 'When night took kingship of the land.' چو is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'; شب is the subject 'night'; در زمین is the locative prepositional phrase 'in/on the earth/land'; پادشاهی گرفت is a light-verb construction where پادشاهی ('kingship, rule') is the thing 'taken' and گرفت is the past of گرفتن 'took/assumed' = 'took/assumed rule.'
segments.s565-g28: ز دریا به دریا سپاهی گرفت = 'he took/gathered an army from sea to sea.' The paired prepositions ز ... به express range/extent ('from X to Y'); سپاهی is the object 'an army' (or 'troops'); گرفت is past tense 'took/gathered.' In Persian poetic phrasing, 'ز دریا به دریا' means across the whole maritime extent, i.e. far and wide.
segments.s565-g29: زمین تیره‌گون کوه چون نیل شد = two coordinated pictorial clauses. زمین تیره‌گون = 'the earth (turned) dark-hued' (noun + adjective); then کوه چون نیل شد = 'the mountain(s) became like indigo' where چون = 'like/as' introduces a simile and شد is the past of شدن 'became.' Word order places the descriptive adjective and then the separate clause for the mountains.
segments.s565-g30: ستاره به کردار قندیل شد = 'the stars became like chandeliers.' ستاره is the subject 'stars.' به here marks likeness/transformative sense 'into / in the manner of,' کردار is used poetically as 'in the manner/guise of' (archaic usage; none of the provided noun senses match modern 'manner' exactly), قندیل = 'chandelier / hanging lamp,' and شد is 'became.'
segments.s565-g31: تو گویی که شمعست سیصدهزار = an idiomatic framing: تو گویی ('you would say' / 'one would say' / 'it seems') introduces a metaphorical comparison. گویی here is a semi-modal/verbal form meaning 'it appears/one would say.' که introduces the content clause. شمعست is a fused copular form (شمع + است) 'is a candle/are candles' and سیصدهزار is the numeral 'three hundred thousand' modifying the implied noun.
segments.s565-g32: بیاویخته ز آسمان حصار = 'hanging/loose-hanging from the enclosure/arch of the sky.' بیاویخته is a participial/adjectival form 'hanging down / suspended.' ز + آسمان = 'from the sky.' حصار here is used poetically for the sky's enclosing arch or ring; in the supplied senses it usually means 'fortress/entrenchment,' but in this context it denotes an encircling vault/arch of heaven (metaphorical).
segments.s565-g17: بگویش is the imperative بگو plus the enclitic -یش (to him/her), so ‘Tell him’. که introduces a reported clause ('that'). با تو ز یک گوهرم places the predicate before the verb: با تو = 'with you / alongside you', ز یک گوهرم = literal 'I am from one gem' i.e. 'I share the same essence/origin as you' (poetic metaphor).
segments.s565-g18: This clause continues the reported content. هم (here) functions as an emphatic 'even/also'. از تخم literally 'from the seed' (metaphorical for lineage/origin). نرسی is the present/subjunctive/negative-shaped form of رسید/رسیدن ('reach/attain') used here with the sense 'reach/attain'. کنداورم appears as a single word in the text; grammatically it reads as a first-person action linked to the clause (I ...), and in the lexicon given it appears as the noun 'excavator' (کنداور). In this line the construction expresses that even from the seed/lineage something will not be attained/reached (i.e. an emphatic denial/protection), with کنداورم functioning as the agentual/relational word bound to that meaning.
segments.s565-g19: همان نیز 'likewise/also' opens the clause. با کین نه is an admonition: با کین 'with hatred/ill will', نه negates it ('do not be with hatred'); note classical word order places the negated predicate after the prepositional phrase. هم گوشه‌ام: هم here reinforces ('also' / 'indeed') and گوشه‌ام (گوشه + -ام) literally 'I am secluded/withdrawn' — here claimed in a negated sense (not withdrawn) by the preceding نه.
segments.s565-g20: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause ('for/that'). خویش توام is idiomatic: خویش = 'one's own/kin', ش is the possessive clitic attached to خوی (here functioning as 'own/of self'), and توام = 'I am yours' (compound poetic form meaning 'I am your own'). دختر نوشه‌ام appends identity/status: دختر 'daughter' and نوشه‌ام (نوشه + -ام) here functions as a descriptive noun (lexical sense 'draught/nectar' is listed but this word can carry a proper-name or figurative role in poetry).
segments.s565-g21: مرا‌گر is a fused conditional phrase meaning 'if (you) want me' (مرا = 'me' placed before the verb for emphasis, گر = 'if'). بخواهی is the subjunctive/present of خواه‌دن 'to want' agreeing with the conditional. حصار آن تست: حصار 'fortress/stronghold', آن 'that', and تست here is a contracted poetic copula (originally 'تو است' = 'it is you' / 'it is yours'); the line states metaphorically that the fortress/defense of 'it' is the addressee.
segments.s565-g22: چو here functions as a conditional/temporal 'if/when'. ایوان 'iwan/porch' is the noun object. بیابی is the subjunctive/optative form of یابیدن 'you may find/you find' (addressed to the same 'you'). نگار 'beloved/ornament' names what adorns the iwan, and again آن تست uses the poetic copula to say 'that (ornament) is you' — a poetic way to identify the addressee with the beauty of the place.
segments.s565-g23: برین کار is a prepositional phrase: بر + این (here orthographically written بر + ین) = 'on this matter/for this affair'. با دایه پیمان کنی is an instruction: با دایه 'with the nurse', پیمان 'covenant/pledge', and کنی (subjunctive/imperative-form of کردن) 'you should make/do' — the verb at the end is typical Persian word order (object(s) preceding verb).
segments.s565-g24: زبان در بزرگی گروگان کنی is a figurative clause: زبان 'word/speech' (here 'your word' implied), در بزرگی 'in (the presence/majesty of) greatness', گروگان 'hostage/pledge', and کنی 'you make/do' — together 'make your speech a hostage in greatness', i.e. give your solemn pledge or bind your word as guarantee before the great.
segments.s565-g1: به + شبگیر is a poetic locative/time phrase meaning ‘at nightfall’ or ‘when night came’ (ز/به used poetically). شاپور یل is a name + epithet (personal name then title/epithet). برنشست is a simple past (third person singular) composed of the preverb بر- + نشست (‘sat’), here meaning ‘sat up’ / ‘rose to a sitting/alert posture’ and links as the main action of the clause.
segments.s565-g2: همی رفت uses the archaic particle همی to mark continued or iterative action (‘kept on / was going’), with رفت the past stem of ‘to go’ used narratively. جوشان is a present-participle/participial adjective modifying the motion or man (‘seething, fervent’). کمانی به دست is an adjectival phrase: کمانی (‘bow-shaped/arched’ or ‘bow-like’) + به دست (‘with in hand’), giving the instrument held as the character moves.
segments.s565-g3: سیه جوشن خسروی is a noun phrase with adjectives preceding the noun in this poetic order: سیه (black) + جوشن (cuirass/armor) + خسروی (kingly/royal). در برش is a locative phrase where در functions as ‘on/over/on his person’ (poetic locution) and برش is the object/area (here understood as ‘on his breast/torso’), so the whole segment describes what he was wearing.
segments.s565-g4: درفشان درفش سیه is a string of modifiers + noun: درفشان (shining/gleaming, used adjectivally) + درفش (banner/standard) + سیه (black). بر سرش is a prepositional phrase: بر ‘on/upon’ + سر ‘head’ + ش (the enclitic 3rd-person possessive = ‘his’), specifying the banner’s position. The clause is nominative/descriptive, giving an image rather than a full finite verb.
segments.s565-g5: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’; دیوار دژ is a genitive/attributive phrase (wall of the fortress / citadel wall). مالکه is the subject (the queen/female ruler). بنگرید is an older/narrative 3rd-person past form of the verb ‘to look/gaze’ (the b- prefix occurs in classical Persian narrative forms), so the segment means ‘the queen looked from the citadel wall.’
segments.s565-g6: This is a simple coordinate clause: درفش ‘the banner/standard’ + و ‘and’ + سر نامداران where سر here functions as ‘chiefs/leaders’ (head/leader) modifying نامداران ‘the famed/renowned ones’. بدید is the past tense ‘saw’ (classical past form), so the queen ‘saw the banner and the leaders of the famed.’
segments.s565-g7: چو is the simile particle ‘like/as’; گل‌رنگ is a compound adjective ‘rose-colored’ modifying رخسار (‘cheek/face’), and the coordinate و + چون introduces a second simile: چون + مشک + موی = ‘hair like musk.’ Word order is adjective (گل‌رنگ) + noun (رخسار) in this poetic phrasing and the two similes are joined by و.
segments.s565-g8: به رنگ is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning ‘in the color/hue of’; طبرخون here is treated as a color-name or proper name of a flower (an epithet), followed by گل (flower) specifying that color source. مشک بوی places two nouns together where مشک (musk) modifies بوی (scent) to mean ‘musk-scented.’ The whole segment gives the precise hue and scent imagery.
segments.s565-g49: بگویش is an imperative/optative form built from بگو + enclitic -ش (tell him/her). که is the complementizer introducing reported speech. گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (classical narrative past) and او is the subject 'he'. به marks the indirect object ('to/unto') and introduces the addressees خورشید و ماه ('the Sun and the Moon'). The sequence follows Persian order: imperative + complementizer + reported-past clause (subject + verb + indirect-object-marking preposition + recipients).
segments.s565-g50: The second به repeats the 'to/for' sense and continues the list of items addressed (زنار 'girdle', زردشت 'Zoroaster', فرخ کلاه 'fortunate/auspicious hat'). The conjunction و links items in a simple coordinate list; فرخ is an adjective/modifier for کلاه. This segment completes the recipients/items of the preceding reported speech.
segments.s565-g51: که again introduces the content of what was said ('that'). هر چیز کز من بخواهی is a relative-like clause meaning 'whatever thing that you ask from me': هر چیز (every/any thing) + کز (poetic form of 'that/from which' = 'that/of which/from which') + من (me) + بخواهی (2nd-person present/subjunctive 'you may want/ask'); همی is an archaic particle often used in epic Persian to mark ongoing/habitual aspect or to soften/continue the verb. Word order is typical: quantifier+noun + relative particle + source + verb.
segments.s565-g52: گر is the conditional 'if'. از پادشاهی بکاهی همی: از marks source/partitive ('of/from the kingship'); بکاهی here means 'a (mere) straw' (کاهی) with the prefix ب- (classical idiom meaning 'even a bit of'), so the clause means 'even if you ask for a straw of kingship' — i.e. even the smallest share. همی again is the archaic particle attached to the verb phrase. The clause is elliptical and depends on the previous 'whatever you ask...'.
segments.s565-g53: ز (poetic form of از) = 'from'. من is the source 'me'. هیچ بد نشنود is a negative wish/optative construction: هیچ بد = 'no evil/anything bad'; نشنود is the present-subjunctive/optative 3rd-person of شنیدن 'to hear' used here as a jussive 'may ... not hear'. گوش تو 'your ear' is the experiencer/possessor (ear of you). The word order places the source first for emphasis (From me no bad may your ear hear).
segments.s565-g54: نجویم is the 1st-person present-subjunctive/optative of جوئیدن/خواستن (with prefixed negative ن- here) and here means 'I will not seek/long for' or 'I do not desire'. جدایی 'separation' is the object of نجویم. ز (from) + آغوش تو = 'from your embrace' (ز = poetic 'from/of'). The clause expresses the speaker's determination not to be parted from the addressee's embrace.
segments.s565-g55: خریدارم is a first-person form built on خریدار (to buy/take) with the suffix -م; here it functions as 'I will buy/take' or 'I am buyer of' in epic diction. او را uses او as direct object with the object marker را. به تخت و کلاه uses به + nouns to indicate the means/price or accompaniment ('with a throne and a hat' or 'for a throne and a hat'): in context it means 'I will secure/obtain him by (giving) a throne and a hat.' The verb-headed clause places the object after the verb (typical Persian VSO/VOS flexibility in poetry).
segments.s565-g56: به فرمان یزدان uses به in its instrumental/agentive sense 'by' (here 'by the command of') — this is a fixed phrase 'by the command of God'. و گنج و سپاه lists additional means/resources (treasure and army) coordinated by و. The whole segment modifies/qualifies how he will 'buy/take' the object: by divine order, with wealth and with troops.
segments.s565-g33: بشد is the past form of شدن used here as 'became' and introduces the predicate that follows. The noun phrase 'دایه لرزان' is the subject ('the nurse, trembling') and 'پر از ترس و بیم' is a compound adjectival/complement phrase: پر (followed by از) means 'full (of)'; از links to the nouns ترس و بیم 'fear and anxiety'. Word order is verb - subject - predicate, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s565-g34: ز طایر is a causal/source prepositional phrase 'because of / on account of the bird'. همی شد is a literary combination: همی (a classical particle) + شد ('became') marking a sudden/standing change of state. دلش ('her heart', دل + enclitic ش) is the subject of the result, بدو is a directional dative 'toward it/him', and نیم is a predicate adjective 'half' — together: 'her heart became half toward it.'
segments.s565-g35: چو آمد is a temporal clause 'when he came' (چو = 'when', آمد = 'came'). به نزدیک is a prepositional/locative phrase 'to/near', and پرده‌سرای is the place-name 'curtain-hall' or 'stage/curtain-chamber' serving as the goal/location of the coming.
segments.s565-g36: خرامید is the past tense of خرامیدن 'to stride/prance proudly' (3rd person). نزدیک functions adverbially 'near/close', and آن پاک‌رای is a noun phrase 'that pure/virtuous one' (آن = 'that', پاک‌رای = epithet 'of pure reputation / virtuous'). The verb precedes the adverbial phrase, a typical poetic order: 'he strode near that virtuous one.'
segments.s565-g37: بدو گفت = 'she said to him' (بدو = to him; گفت = said). اگر نزد شاهم بری is a conditional clause: اگر = 'if', نزد = 'in the presence of / before', شاهم = 'my king' (شاه + enclitic م), and بری here is an archaic/poetic subjunctive/imperative form meaning 'you go' (i.e. 'if you go before my king'). Note the older verbal form بری (not the modern بروی) is used in epic diction.
segments.s565-g38: بیابی is a 2nd-person verb meaning 'you will find / you will obtain' (subjunctive/future sense here as result of the condition). ز من = 'from me', and تاج و انگشتری are the direct objects 'a crown and a ring' (تاج = crown/honor; انگشتری = ring). The clause expresses a promise: 'you will find from me a crown and a ring.'
segments.s565-g39: هشیوار سالار is a noun phrase 'the watchful/alert commander' (هشیوار = alert; سالار = commander/chief). بارش ببرد is a verbal phrase: بار + enclitic ش = 'his burden/load', and ببرد is a past/perfective form of بردن here meaning 'he took/carried (away)'. So: 'the watchful commander took his load.'
segments.s565-g40: ز دهلیز indicates source 'from the corridor'. پرده بر شاه گرد uses پرده 'curtain' + بر ('toward / before') + گرد, here an archaic verbal use related to گردیدن 'to turn/bring about' — poetically '(was) turned/brought toward the king.' The line thus says the curtain was brought/turned from the corridor to the king's presence (word order is compact and archaic).

sentences.s565-s29: When he heard the reply, he immediately came forth from behind the curtain, running toward the fortress.
sentences.s565-s30: Having heard that, the silvery cypress thereupon said that the Sun had become a mate for Nahid.
sentences.s565-s31: From above and at (the) meeting with King Shapur, he related what had befallen the shining moon.
sentences.s565-s21: She came, and lowering her lashes to the ground, she told the king whatever words she had heard.
sentences.s565-s22: The king became glad at her words; he laughed and gave her a thousand dinars.
sentences.s565-s23: Two attendants—one (received) a collar and a ring—made of Chinese brocade and of Barbarian (cloth).
sentences.s565-s24: She gave this reply: “With a moon‑bright face, speak many words well.”
sentences.s565-s5: Sleep and calm came upon that fair‑faced one; the nurse was left with a heart full of affection.
sentences.s565-s6: She said to him: 'This is the king Khurshidfash — one who has come here so vindictive.'
sentences.s565-s7: His greatness is, as it were, concealed and well‑fitting; where is the world I could call him — where is a world worthy of him?
sentences.s565-s8: A message from me reached Shapur; he has come to the battle, and on my account he held a feast.
sentences.s565-s13: He said to the nurse, "Tell me whatever you command; I'll tell him and bring you to him so you can learn from him."
sentences.s565-s14: When night took kingship over the earth, he mustered an army from sea to sea.
sentences.s565-s15: The earth grew dark; the mountains became like indigo; the stars became like chandeliers.
sentences.s565-s16: You would say that they were three hundred thousand candles, hanging from the vault of the sky.
sentences.s565-s9: Tell him that I am of the same essence as you; I will not allow (it) even from the seed to be reached by the digger.
sentences.s565-s10: Likewise, be not wrathful; I am not withdrawn — I am your own, a daughter (and) a draught.
sentences.s565-s11: If you desire me, its fortress is you; if you find an iwan, its ornament (beloved) is you.
sentences.s565-s12: On this matter make a covenant with the nurse; place your tongue as a hostage in greatness (i.e., pledge your word solemnly).
sentences.s565-s1: At nightfall Shapur the hero sat up; he kept going, seething, with a bow in his hand.
sentences.s565-s2: He wore the kingly black cuirass on his chest; a gleaming black standard was (held) above his head.
sentences.s565-s3: From the citadel wall the queen looked and saw the standard and the leaders of the famed.
sentences.s565-s4: Her cheek like a rose and her hair like musk — in the hue of the tabrakhun flower and with the scent of musk.
sentences.s565-s25: Tell him that he said to the Sun and Moon, to the girdle, to Zoroaster, and to the fortunate hat.
sentences.s565-s26: That whatever thing you ask of me— even if you ask for a straw of the kingship.
sentences.s565-s27: May your ear hear no evil from me; I will not seek separation from your embrace.
sentences.s565-s28: I will buy/take him with throne and hat, by the command of God, and with treasure and an army.
sentences.s565-s17: The nurse became trembling, filled with fear and anxiety; because of the bird her heart became half inclined toward it.
sentences.s565-s18: When he came near the curtain-hall, he strode close to that virtuous one.
sentences.s565-s19: She said to him, 'If you go before my king, you will obtain from me a crown and a ring.'
sentences.s565-s20: The alert commander took up his burden, and from the corridor brought the curtain toward the king.
